enrichment.short_description: ReadMe is an API documentation platform with a WYSIWYG editor, interactive API reference, and AI-assisted doc writing.
enrichment.starting_price: Free (Starter plan); Pro plans from $250/month billed annually
enrichment.seo_meta_description: ReadMe is an API documentation platform with a free Starter plan, interactive API reference, Git sync, and AI-assisted doc writing.

features.title: WYSIWYG Editor
features.description: Edit documentation directly in the browser without needing to write raw markdown or code.

features.title: Bi-Directional Git Sync
features.description: Keeps docs in sync with GitHub, GitHub Enterprise Server, or GitLab repositories in both directions.

features.title: Interactive API Reference
features.description: Auto-generates an interactive, testable API reference from an OpenAPI spec.

features.title: Visual API Designer
features.description: Build and edit an API definition visually without writing a spec by hand.

features.title: AI Writer
features.description: Analyzes pull requests and drafts documentation updates when underlying code changes.

features.title: AI Linter
features.description: Enforces a custom style guide automatically across documentation pages.

features.title: Branching & Reviews
features.description: Branch-based collaboration workflow for proposing and reviewing documentation changes.

features.title: MCP Server
features.description: Exposes ReadMe documentation to AI assistants and agents such as Claude and Cursor.

pros: Free Starter plan includes a custom domain, bi-directional Git sync, and an interactive API reference.
AI Writer drafts documentation updates automatically from pull requests.
Branch-based review workflow lets teams collaborate on docs like code.
Used by companies including NVIDIA, Amazon, and PagerDuty.
cons: Branching, private docs, and most AI features require the paid Pro plan ($250/month billed annually).
Enterprise plan pricing starts at $3,000+/month for multi-project support and SSO.
Ask AI is a separate $150/month add-on rather than included in the Pro plan.

faqs.question: Does ReadMe have a free plan?
faqs.answer: Yes, the Starter plan is free and includes a custom domain, bi-directional Git sync, and an interactive API reference.

faqs.question: What does the Pro plan cost?
faqs.answer: Pro is billed at $250/month on an annual plan and adds branching and reviews, private docs, the AI Linter, and GitHub AI Writer.

faqs.question: Can ReadMe generate documentation from an OpenAPI spec?
faqs.answer: Yes, it supports OpenAPI specs with an interactive API explorer, plus a Visual API Designer for building docs without a spec.

faqs.question: Does ReadMe integrate with GitHub and GitLab?
faqs.answer: Yes, it offers bi-directional Git sync with GitHub, GitHub Enterprise Server, and GitLab.

faqs.question: Does ReadMe support single sign-on?
faqs.answer: SSO through Microsoft Entra ID, Google Workspace, or Okta is available on the Enterprise plan.

use_cases.title: Public API documentation
use_cases.description: Publish an interactive, branded API reference generated from an OpenAPI spec.
use_cases.audience: API-first companies and developer relations teams

use_cases.title: Docs that update with code
use_cases.description: Use the AI Writer to draft documentation changes automatically when a pull request changes API behavior.

features: Ask AI Lite, AI Linter, GitHub AI Writer

long_form_blocks.heading: What is ReadMe?
paragraphs: ReadMe is a developer-facing documentation platform for publishing API references, guides, and changelogs. It combines a browser-based WYSIWYG editor with bi-directional Git sync, so teams can edit docs visually or keep them in sync with a GitHub or GitLab repository.

The platform builds an interactive API reference from an OpenAPI spec and includes AI features such as an AI Writer that drafts documentation updates from pull requests and an AI Linter that enforces a style guide across pages.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who ReadMe is for
paragraphs: ReadMe suits API-first companies and developer relations teams that need a public or private API reference, and engineering teams that want documentation changes tracked alongside code through branching and pull-request-driven AI drafts.

enrichment.short_description: Mintlify is a documentation platform with a web editor, AI chat, and an MCP server, used by companies including Anthropic and Coinbase.
enrichment.starting_price: Free (Starter plan); Pro and Enterprise plans available
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Mintlify offers a free Starter plan plus Pro/Enterprise tiers for building AI-ready, self-updating documentation.

features.title: Web Editor
features.description: Build and edit documentation directly in the browser with a custom domain.

features.title: MCP Server
features.description: Exposes documentation through the Model Context Protocol so AI agents can read it.

features.title: API Playground
features.description: Interactive API testing embedded directly in the documentation.

features.title: AI Chat / Assistant
features.description: Lets readers ask questions answered from the documentation content (Pro and above).

features.title: Agent Score
features.description: Assesses how well AI agents can read and use a site's documentation.

features.title: Automations & Preview Deployments
features.description: Automated workflows and preview builds available on the Pro plan.

features.title: SSO / SCIM / RBAC
features.description: Enterprise-grade access controls available on the Enterprise plan.

pros: Free Starter plan includes full platform access, a custom domain, and an MCP server.
Reports over 20,000 companies as users, including Anthropic, Amazon, AT&T, and Coinbase.
States 99.99% uptime across all services.
Agent-native features (MCP server, Agent Score) are built specifically for AI-agent-readable docs.
cons: Pro plan pricing is not listed publicly on the site; requires sign-up to see cost.
AI credits (10,000/month on Starter) incur overage charges of $0.01 per credit beyond the included amount.
Enterprise features such as SSO, SCIM, and RBAC require contacting sales.

faqs.question: Is Mintlify free?
faqs.answer: Yes, the Starter plan is free and includes full platform access, a custom domain, and an MCP server.

faqs.question: What is Mintlify's Agent Score?
faqs.answer: It's a feature that assesses whether AI agents can effectively read and use a site's documentation.

faqs.question: Does Mintlify support single sign-on?
faqs.answer: SSO, SCIM, and RBAC are available on the Enterprise plan.

faqs.question: How many AI credits come with the free plan?
faqs.answer: The Starter plan includes 10,000 AI credits per month, with overages billed at $0.01 per credit.

use_cases.title: Agent-readable API docs
use_cases.description: Publish documentation optimized for both human readers and AI agents via the MCP server and Agent Score.

use_cases.title: Enterprise documentation governance
use_cases.description: Manage access control and security for internal and external docs using SSO, SCIM, and RBAC.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Mintlify?
paragraphs: Mintlify is a documentation platform built around a browser-based web editor, an MCP server, and an API playground. It positions itself as an 'agent-native' platform, adding features such as AI chat over the documentation and an Agent Score that measures how well AI agents can read a site's docs.

The Starter plan is free and includes full platform access, a custom domain, and 10,000 AI credits per month. Paid Pro and Enterprise plans add automations, admin APIs, and enterprise access controls such as SSO, SCIM, and RBAC.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Mintlify is for
paragraphs: Mintlify fits startups and developer platform teams that want a free, hosted documentation site with AI chat, and enterprises that need SSO, SCIM, and RBAC for internal and external documentation.

enrichment.short_description: Fern generates API documentation, SDKs, and CLIs from OpenAPI, GraphQL, AsyncAPI, and gRPC specs, and operates as a Postman company.
enrichment.starting_price: Free (Hobby plan); Team plan from $150/month ($112.50/month billed annually)
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Fern turns API specs into documentation, SDKs, and CLIs, with a free Hobby plan and paid Team/Enterprise tiers.

features.title: Docs as Code
features.description: Manage documentation alongside source code using git-based workflows.

features.title: SDK Generation
features.description: Generates client libraries from OpenAPI, GraphQL, AsyncAPI, and gRPC specifications.

features.title: Built-in AI Chat
features.description: Lets documentation readers ask questions answered from the docs content.

features.title: MCP Server
features.description: Exposes documentation and APIs through the Model Context Protocol.

features.title: llms.txt Generation
features.description: Auto-generates machine-readable markdown so LLMs and agents can consume the docs.

features.title: Multi-Source Aggregation
features.description: Combines documentation content from multiple repositories into one site.

features.title: Self-Hosting Option
features.description: Deploy documentation on Fern's cloud or self-host it.

features.title: Postman Integration
features.description: Natively generates and publishes Postman collections.

pros: Free Hobby plan includes guides, an API reference, changelogs, an API explorer, and 250 AI credits per month.
SOC 2 Type II certified with annual penetration tests.
Customers include ElevenLabs, Auth0, NVIDIA, and Deepgram.
Now backed by Postman, extending access to Postman's collection tooling.
cons: Team plan pricing ($150/month) is per team and caps at 5 members before requiring Enterprise.
Enterprise features like SSO and self-hosting require a custom, sales-negotiated plan.
Fern was acquired by Postman, which could affect long-term product direction.

faqs.question: Is Fern free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, the Hobby plan is free forever and includes 2 team members and 250 AI credits per month.

faqs.question: What does the Team plan cost?
faqs.answer: $150/month billed monthly, or $112.50/month billed annually, for up to 5 team members and 1,000 AI credits.

faqs.question: Is Fern part of Postman?
faqs.answer: Yes, Fern's website states it is 'a Postman company' following an acquisition.

faqs.question: Can Fern generate SDKs?
faqs.answer: Yes, it generates client libraries from OpenAPI, GraphQL, AsyncAPI, and gRPC specs.

use_cases.title: Multi-language SDK generation
use_cases.description: Generate client SDKs across languages directly from an existing API spec.

use_cases.title: Free hosted API docs
use_cases.description: Publish a documentation site with guides, an API reference, and a changelog at no cost on the Hobby plan.

features: Guides, API refs, changelogs, API explorer, web editor

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Fern?
paragraphs: Fern generates API documentation, SDKs, and CLIs from specifications written in OpenAPI, GraphQL, AsyncAPI, or gRPC. It manages docs as code with git-based workflows and includes a built-in AI chat so readers can ask questions answered from the documentation itself.
Fern is now described on its site as 'a Postman company' following an acquisition, and it natively supports generating and publishing Postman collections.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Fern is for
paragraphs: Fern fits API platform teams that want auto-generated, multi-language SDKs from a spec, and startups or individual developers who want a free hosted documentation site with guides, an API reference, and a changelog.

enrichment.short_description: Speakeasy generates SDKs and CLIs from OpenAPI specs, and offers a separate AI control plane for governing AI agent access to company systems.
enrichment.starting_price: Free (SDK & MCP generation, 250 operations/language); Business plan from $720/month ($600/month billed annually)
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Speakeasy generates SDKs and MCP servers from OpenAPI specs, with a free tier, paid Business/Enterprise plans, and an AI control plane product.

features.title: SDK & MCP Generation
features.description: Generates client SDKs and Model Context Protocol servers from OpenAPI specs.

features.title: Auto-Publish
features.description: Publishes generated SDKs automatically to a package manager.

features.title: OAuth & Webhooks
features.description: Adds OAuth support, webhooks, and SSE to generated SDKs (Business plan).

features.title: AI Control Plane
features.description: A control layer for connecting AI agents to SaaS tools, APIs, and internal systems with policy enforcement.

features.title: Real-Time Inspection
features.description: Blocks PII, secrets, and prompt-injection attempts in AI agent traffic.

features.title: Pre-Built Integrations
features.description: Includes Salesforce, Slack, HubSpot, and identity providers such as Okta and Entra ID.

features.title: Usage Observability
features.description: Tracks AI agent usage by team, tool, and user with a full audit trail.

pros: Free tier for SDK/MCP generation includes 250 operations per language and community Slack support.
Customers retain ownership of generated code even after cancelling a plan.
SOC 2 Type II and ISO 27001 certified.
Supports MCP-based agents including Claude, ChatGPT, Cursor, and GitHub Copilot.
cons: Business plan is priced per licensed programming language, which adds up for multi-language SDK support.
The AI Control Plane product is Enterprise-only with 'tailored' pricing that is not public.
Founding year, headquarters, and company size are not published on the site.

faqs.question: Does Speakeasy have a free plan?
faqs.answer: Yes, the SDK & CLI free tier includes SDK/MCP generation with 250 operations per language.

faqs.question: What does the Business plan cost?
faqs.answer: $720/month per licensed language, or $600/month billed annually, with a free trial available.

faqs.question: What happens to generated SDK code if I cancel?
faqs.answer: Speakeasy states customers own 100% of the code already generated, though new code can no longer be generated without an active plan.

faqs.question: Does Speakeasy support MCP-based AI agents?
faqs.answer: Yes, it supports Claude, ChatGPT, Cursor, GitHub Copilot, and other MCP-compatible agents.

use_cases.title: Multi-language SDK generation
use_cases.description: Generate type-safe client SDKs and CLIs directly from an OpenAPI spec.

use_cases.title: AI agent access governance
use_cases.description: Control and audit which internal systems and SaaS tools AI agents can reach.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Speakeasy?
paragraphs: Speakeasy started as an SDK and CLI generation tool: it turns an OpenAPI spec into type-safe client SDKs and MCP servers, with a free tier for a single language and paid Business/Enterprise plans for teams needing OAuth support, webhooks, and dedicated support.

The company has since added a second product, an AI Control Plane, which acts as a control layer for connecting AI agents such as Claude, ChatGPT, and Cursor to a company's SaaS tools, APIs, and internal systems, with policy enforcement, real-time inspection for PII/secrets, and usage observability.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Speakeasy is for
paragraphs: API platform engineers use Speakeasy to auto-generate SDKs and MCP servers from their OpenAPI spec, while enterprise security and IT teams use the AI Control Plane to govern and audit which internal systems AI agents can access.

enrichment.short_description: Bump.sh publishes API documentation portals and deploys MCP servers from OpenAPI and AsyncAPI specs, with automatic breaking-change detection.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Bump.sh turns OpenAPI and AsyncAPI specs into hosted documentation and MCP servers, with plans starting at $50/month.

features.title: API Documentation Portals
features.description: Generates branded, searchable documentation from OpenAPI, AsyncAPI, and Arazzo specs.

features.title: Diff & Preview on PRs
features.description: Shows API spec differences and a live preview on every pull request.

features.title: MCP Servers
features.description: Deploys managed or on-premise MCP servers directly from API specs.

features.title: Breaking Change Detection
features.description: Automatically flags breaking changes before deployment.

features.title: Automatic Changelog
features.description: Generates a changelog on every release (Pro plan and above).

features.title: Usage Analytics
features.description: Shows logs, debug sessions, and reports on how developers and agents use the API.

features.title: CLI & GitHub Integration
features.description: Integrates with git-based CI/CD workflows.

pros: Automatically detects breaking changes and generates changelogs on release.
Deploys managed MCP servers directly from OpenAPI/AsyncAPI specs.
Pro plan includes a 14-day free trial.
Open-source projects can request free access to the Pro plan.
cons: No permanent free plan; the Basic plan starts at $50/month.
Basic plan caps usage at 10 API docs, 5 MCP tools, and 20 external users.
On-premise deployment and SSO require the Custom plan with contact-sales pricing.

faqs.question: Does Bump.sh have a free plan?
faqs.answer: No, but the Pro plan includes a 14-day free trial, and open-source projects can request free access.

faqs.question: What does the Basic plan include?
faqs.answer: $50/month for up to 10 API docs, 5 MCP tools, 3 internal users, and 20 external users.

faqs.question: Can Bump.sh detect breaking API changes?
faqs.answer: Yes, it automatically flags breaking changes and shows diffs and previews on pull requests.

faqs.question: Can Bump.sh generate MCP servers?
faqs.answer: Yes, it deploys managed or on-premise MCP servers from OpenAPI, AsyncAPI, and Arazzo specs.

use_cases.title: Branded API documentation portals
use_cases.description: Publish searchable, branded API docs with CI-based deployment from a git repository.

use_cases.title: Hosted MCP servers from existing specs
use_cases.description: Generate managed MCP servers directly from OpenAPI/AsyncAPI specs to support AI agent integrations.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Bump.sh?
paragraphs: Bump.sh publishes hosted API documentation portals from OpenAPI and AsyncAPI specifications, showing a diff and preview on every pull request and flagging breaking changes automatically before deployment.
The platform also deploys MCP (Model Context Protocol) servers generated from the same specs, plus usage analytics showing how developers and AI agents interact with the API.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Bump.sh is for
paragraphs: Bump.sh fits platform and API teams that want CI-driven, branded documentation portals, and teams that want a managed MCP server generated directly from their existing OpenAPI or AsyncAPI spec.

enrichment.short_description: Redocly is an API documentation platform (Realm) built around the open-source Redoc renderer, with plans starting at $10 per seat/month.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Redocly's Realm platform builds API docs from OpenAPI and AsyncAPI specs, starting at $10/seat/month with a 30-day free trial.

features.title: Reunite
features.description: Collaborative tool for API design review with side-by-side visual diffs.

features.title: Redoc
features.description: Open-source API reference generator supporting OpenAPI, GraphQL, AsyncAPI, and SOAP.

features.title: Revel
features.description: Developer hub for API onboarding and analytics.

features.title: Reef
features.description: Internal API catalog and governance tool.

features.title: Mock Servers
features.description: Generates mock servers directly from API definitions.

features.title: Remote Content
features.description: Single-sources content for reuse across multiple documentation sets.

features.title: AI Search
features.description: Available on the Enterprise plan for searching across documentation.

features.title: MCP Servers
features.description: Available on the Enterprise plan for exposing docs to AI agents.

pros: Redoc, the underlying open-source renderer, has over 1M weekly npm downloads and is free to use standalone.
Realm platform reports over 212,000 companies as users and 1B+ APIs linted.
30-day free trial on both Pro and Enterprise plans.
Modular add-ons (Revel, Reef) let teams pay only for the products they need.
cons: No permanent free tier for the hosted Realm platform; Pro starts at $10/seat/month.
Enterprise features (SSO, RBAC, AI search, MCP servers) require the $24/seat/month Enterprise plan.
Add-on products (Revel, Reef) cost extra per seat on top of the base plan.

faqs.question: Is Redoc free?
faqs.answer: The open-source Redoc renderer is free to use standalone; the hosted Realm platform (Pro/Enterprise) is a paid product with a 30-day free trial.

faqs.question: What does the Pro plan cost?
faqs.answer: $10 per seat/month, billed monthly, including 1 project and 100 pages.

faqs.question: What's included in Enterprise?
faqs.answer: $24 per seat/month, adding SSO, RBAC, AI search, and MCP servers on top of Pro features.

faqs.question: Where is Redocly headquartered?
faqs.answer: Austin, Texas.

use_cases.title: Free OpenAPI reference rendering
use_cases.description: Embed the open-source Redoc renderer to display an interactive API reference at no cost.

use_cases.title: API documentation governance
use_cases.description: Manage collaborative review, an internal API catalog, and governance across many APIs.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Redocly?
paragraphs: Redocly is an API documentation platform, called Realm, built around the open-source Redoc renderer. Redoc itself is free and widely used (over 1M weekly npm downloads) to render interactive API references from OpenAPI, GraphQL, AsyncAPI, or SOAP definitions.

The commercial Realm platform adds collaborative review (Reunite), a developer hub (Revel), an internal API catalog (Reef), mock servers, and enterprise features such as SSO, RBAC, and AI search. Plans start at $10/seat/month with a 30-day free trial.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Redocly is for
paragraphs: Individual developers can use the free, open-source Redoc renderer directly. Platform and API governance teams managing many APIs across an organization are the primary audience for the paid Realm platform.

name: SwaggerHub
skip_reason: Product has been rebranded away from the 'SwaggerHub' name. SmartBear renamed SwaggerHub to 'API Hub' and has since rebranded again to 'Swagger' / 'Swagger Studio' (confirmed via SmartBear's own materials: 'API Hub is now Swagger'). The listed website (swagger.io/tools/swaggerhub) now shows the broader rebranded 'Swagger' platform (Studio, Portal, Explore, Contract Testing) rather than a distinct SwaggerHub product, and the pricing page does not render plan details for WebFetch, so content specific to a still-operating 'SwaggerHub' product cannot be verified.

enrichment.short_description: Docusaurus is a free, open-source static site generator for building documentation sites with Markdown/MDX and React, maintained by Meta.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Docusaurus is Meta's free, open-source static site generator for building documentation sites with Markdown, MDX, and React.

features.title: MDX Support
features.description: Write docs and blog posts in MDX, which Docusaurus compiles into static HTML.

features.title: React Customization
features.description: Extend and customize a site's layout by writing React components.

features.title: Built-in Localization
features.description: Out-of-the-box internationalization using git, Crowdin, or any translation manager.

features.title: Document Versioning
features.description: Supports maintaining documentation for multiple versions of a project at once.

features.title: Algolia Search Integration
features.description: Official built-in support for Algolia documentation search.

features.title: Built-in Blog
features.description: Includes a blogging feature alongside standard documentation pages.

pros: Completely free and open source, maintained by Meta Open Source.
Built-in versioning lets projects support docs for multiple releases at once.
Out-of-the-box localization workflow via git or Crowdin.
Official Algolia search integration.
cons: Deeper customization requires familiarity with React and MDX.
Self-hosted — teams must handle their own build and deployment.
No built-in AI features.

faqs.question: Is Docusaurus free?
faqs.answer: Yes, it's a free, open-source project maintained by Meta.

faqs.question: Does Docusaurus support multiple languages?
faqs.answer: Yes, it has built-in localization support using git, Crowdin, or other translation managers.

faqs.question: Can I version my documentation with Docusaurus?
faqs.answer: Yes, it supports maintaining documentation for multiple versions of a project simultaneously.

faqs.question: Does Docusaurus support search?
faqs.answer: Yes, it officially supports Algolia documentation search.

use_cases.title: Versioned open-source docs
use_cases.description: Publish documentation that tracks multiple released versions of a project.

use_cases.title: Customizable docs sites
use_cases.description: Build a documentation site with custom React components and layouts.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Docusaurus?
paragraphs: Docusaurus is a free, open-source static site generator maintained under Meta Open Source. It converts Markdown and MDX content into static HTML websites and lets teams customize layouts with React components.
Built-in features include document versioning for supporting multiple releases at once, out-of-the-box localization via git or Crowdin, and official Algolia search integration.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Docusaurus is for
paragraphs: Docusaurus suits open-source projects that need versioned, searchable documentation, and developer teams or technical writers who want a customizable, React-based documentation site.

enrichment.short_description: MkDocs is a free, open-source static site generator that builds project documentation from Markdown files and a single YAML config file.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: MkDocs is a free, open-source static site generator for project documentation, created in 2014 and configured via one YAML file.

features.title: Markdown-Based Authoring
features.description: Write documentation content entirely in Markdown.

features.title: Single YAML Config
features.description: Configure navigation, theme, and plugins through one mkdocs.yml file.

features.title: Live Preview
features.description: Preview the site while working, with auto-reload on save.

features.title: Host Anywhere
features.description: Builds static HTML deployable to GitHub Pages, Amazon S3, or any static host.

features.title: Built-in Themes
features.description: Ships with 'mkdocs' and 'readthedocs' themes, plus support for third-party themes.

features.title: Plugin & Extension Support
features.description: Extensible via Markdown extensions and plugins.

pros: Completely free and open source.
Simple, single-file YAML configuration is easy to learn.
Deployable to any static host, including GitHub Pages and Amazon S3.
Supports custom and third-party themes.
cons: Built-in theming is more limited than some design-focused generators.
No built-in AI or hosted search features.
Requires Python tooling to build and serve the site.

faqs.question: Is MkDocs free?
faqs.answer: Yes, it's free and open source.

faqs.question: When was MkDocs created?
faqs.answer: It was originally created by Tom Christie in 2014.

faqs.question: Where can I host a MkDocs site?
faqs.answer: Anywhere that serves static HTML, including GitHub Pages and Amazon S3.

faqs.question: Does MkDocs support themes?
faqs.answer: Yes, it ships with built-in 'mkdocs' and 'readthedocs' themes and supports custom or third-party themes.

use_cases.title: Python project documentation
use_cases.description: Document a Python library or tool using Markdown and a single config file.

use_cases.title: Lightweight docs sites
use_cases.description: Build a config-file-driven documentation site without a heavier framework.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is MkDocs?
paragraphs: MkDocs is a free, open-source static site generator, originally created by Tom Christie in 2014, geared toward building project documentation. Content is written in Markdown and the site is configured through a single mkdocs.yml file.

It builds static HTML that can be hosted anywhere, including GitHub Pages or Amazon S3, and ships with built-in 'mkdocs' and 'readthedocs' themes alongside support for custom and third-party themes and plugins.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who MkDocs is for
paragraphs: MkDocs fits open-source maintainers and developer teams who want a lightweight, Markdown-based documentation site driven by a single configuration file rather than a heavier framework.

enrichment.short_description: VitePress is a free, open-source static site generator built on Vite and Vue for creating documentation sites from Markdown.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: VitePress is a free, MIT-licensed static site generator built on Vite and Vue for Markdown-based documentation sites.

features.title: Markdown-Focused Authoring
features.description: Write content in Markdown with Vue components embedded directly.

features.title: Vite-Powered Dev Server
features.description: Instant server start and fast hot-module updates during development.

features.title: Custom Vue Themes
features.description: Build fully custom site themes using Vue components.

features.title: Static HTML Output
features.description: Pre-renders static HTML for fast initial load, then uses client-side routing after load.

features.title: Built-in Internationalization
features.description: Supports documentation and site content in multiple languages.

features.title: llms.txt Support
features.description: Provides /llms.txt and /llms-full.txt endpoints for LLM-optimized documentation access.

pros: Free and open source under the MIT License.
Built on Vite, giving a fast dev server start and hot updates.
Maintained under the official Vue.js GitHub organization.
Provides llms.txt/llms-full.txt for LLM- and agent-friendly documentation access.
cons: Best suited for teams already using Vue; less of a native fit for non-Vue stacks.
No built-in AI features beyond the llms.txt export.
Requires Node.js/Vite tooling knowledge to customize themes.

faqs.question: Is VitePress free?
faqs.answer: Yes, it's free and open source under the MIT License.

faqs.question: What is VitePress built on?
faqs.answer: Vite and Vue.

faqs.question: Does VitePress support internationalization?
faqs.answer: Yes, its documentation is available in English, Chinese, Portuguese, Russian, Spanish, Korean, Persian, and Japanese.

faqs.question: Does VitePress work with AI tools or LLMs?
faqs.answer: It provides /llms.txt and /llms-full.txt endpoints for LLM-optimized access to its documentation.

use_cases.title: Vue ecosystem documentation
use_cases.description: Build fast, Vue-native documentation sites for libraries and projects.

use_cases.title: Markdown-first docs with custom theming
use_cases.description: Create a lightweight documentation site with custom Vue-based themes.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is VitePress?
paragraphs: VitePress is a free, open-source static site generator built on Vite and Vue, released under the MIT License and maintained under the official Vue.js GitHub organization. It turns Markdown content into documentation sites, with Vue components usable directly inside the Markdown.

It benefits from Vite's fast dev server and hot-module updates, pre-renders static HTML for fast initial loads, and supports custom themes built with Vue. It also exposes /llms.txt and /llms-full.txt for LLM-optimized documentation access.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who VitePress is for
paragraphs: VitePress fits Vue and JavaScript developers documenting libraries or projects in the Vue ecosystem, and open-source maintainers who want a lightweight, Markdown-first docs generator with custom theming.
